headline: Manipur, with a population of 3.1 million, opened more PMJDY accounts per capita than every metro state.
statLabel: PMJDY ACCOUNTS / CAPITA · MN > MAHARASHTRA · DEC 2025
lede: Three of the eight North-Eastern states sit in the top six for per-capita PMJDY enrolment — every quarter for five years running. The lift is not from a single state's surge; it is structural.
source: SOURCE · SLBC NORTH-EAST · 39 QUARTERS · n = 16 DISTRICTS

headline: East Garo Hills has a higher CD ratio (71.4%) than Bengaluru Urban — and one-tenth the deposit base.
statLabel: CREDIT-DEPOSIT RATIO · EAST GARO HILLS · DEC 2025
lede: Five of Meghalaya's twelve districts now sit above the all-India CD-ratio median. Eight years ago, only one did. The story is not about deposits flooding in — it is about credit catching up.
source: SOURCE · SLBC MEGHALAYA · 14TH MEETING · n = 12 DISTRICTS

headline: UPI transaction value in the eight North-East states crossed ₹1 lakh crore for the first time in Q4 FY25.
statLabel: UPI VALUE · 8 NE STATES · QoQ Q4 FY25
lede: Tripura and Mizoram led, each posting more than 22% quarter-on-quarter growth. The growth is concentrated in tier-2 and tier-3 districts, not the state capitals — a quiet inversion of the earlier UPI story.
source: SOURCE · PHONEPE PULSE · Q4 FY25 · n = 89 DISTRICTS

headline: West Bengal's Murshidabad opened more KCC cards in 2024 than the entire state of Sikkim has open since the scheme began.
lede: The KCC scheme tracks the geography of small-farmer agriculture, not banking density. The contrast doesn't reveal a bank story — it reveals where India's farmer-borrowers are.

headline: Kerala has more bank branches per capita than any other major state — and the lowest CD ratio.
statDelta: ▼ DESPITE 1.8× THE BRANCH DENSITY OF GUJARAT
lede: Kerala's deposit base — fed by remittances — outruns its credit demand. More branches collect more savings, but that money flows out to fund lending elsewhere. A net-saver state.
source: SOURCE · SLBC KERALA · 23 QUARTERS · n = 14 DISTRICTS

headline: Aadhaar enrolments in the second half of 2025 were 2.2× higher than the first half — concentrated in just three states.
lede: After years of saturating, enrolment surged again — driven almost entirely by children turning five. The story tracks demographics, not adult inclusion.
